content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 7, 2016

Michael A. Guina
City Attorney
1333 Park Avenue
Emeryville, CA 94608-3517

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-002

Dear Mr. Guina:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Emeryville Mayor Dianne
Martinez regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") does not act as the finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Mayor Martinez from taking part in
decisions to amend the City's General Plan and Pedestrian and Bicycle plan to add a pedestrian path
between 45th and 47th Streets that is within 500 feet of her residence?

CONCLUSION

Mayor Martinez is not disqualified from decisions regarding the pedestrian path between
45th and 47th Streets because it is unlikely that this small improvement in her neighborhood's
walkability will have a measurable impact on her residence.

FACTS

Dianne Martinez is the Mayor for the City of Emeryville. She was elected to the City
Council in the November 2014 election, and was appointed Mayor by the City Council on

1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-002
Page No. 2

December 1, 2015. The City of Emeryville is located in the San Francisco Bay Area and surrounded
by the Bay and the cities of Oakland and Berkeley. It is approximately one square mile of usable
land. Emeryville is an urban locality, comprised primarily of residential, commercial, and industrial
uses. The residential population is approximately 10,000.

The City's General Plan calls for a number of mid-block pedestrian paths to promote
pedestrian access throughout the City. The General Plan describes pedestrian paths as "exclusive
walkways for pedestrians" and prohibits bicycles and motor vehicles on the paths. Goals and
policies of the General Plan encourage the development of mid-block crossings to enhance the
City's pedestrian system. The General Plan includes a Pedestrian System Map, which depicts
various types of pedestrian routes. The City's Pedestrian and Bicycle Plan provide a blueprint for
improving pedestrian and bicycle infrastructure and programs in the City. Its stated purpose is to
complement the General Plan and guide the implementation of General Plan policies that support
walking and bicycling.

The Pedestrian and Bicycle Plan include a map titled "Recommended Pedestrian
Improvements," which shows existing and proposed pedestrian paths. The plan also includes a
"Detailed Pedestrian Project List," which describes various proposed pedestrian improvements.
Relevant to this analysis, in 2011, the map previously depicted a pedestrian path between 45th and
47th Streets in the general vicinity of the EBI campus, a private school for grades Pre-K to 8.
Likewise, a table listed a "North-South pedestrian path from 45th St to 47th St east of San Pablo in
Triangle Neighborhood."

On May 19, 2011, the City Council approved a Conditional Use Permit to establish the EBI
campus in an existing 28,000 square foot building located at 4550 San Pablo Avenue. One of the
conditions of approval of this project was the dedication, design, and construction of a mid-block
pedestrian path ("Path") connecting 45th Street and 47th Street along the eastern property line of
the school campus. The construction of the Path was consistent with the General Plan and
Pedestrian and Bicycle Plan then in effect.

On September 27, 2012, the Planning Commission approved the design of the Path.
However, neighbors opposed to the project appealed this decision, and on December 4, 2012, the
City Council considered the appeal, granting it in part and denying it in part. Specifically, the
Council upheld the Planning Commission's decision to approve the Path construction and design,
but granted the portion of the appeal requesting a change in materials used to construct the Path. As
part of this consideration, the City Council directed staff to initiate a General Plan Amendment to
delete depiction of a pedestrian path on the General Plan, as well as deleting references to a
pedestrian path from the Pedestrian and Bicycle Plan. Thereafter the City Council adopted those
amendments to the General Plan and the Pedestrian and Bicycle Plan.

Although the City Council deleted the path depiction from the General Plan and the
Pedestrian and Bicycle Plan, the conditions of approval for EBI's use permit still require the school
to construct the Path prior to the issuance of certificate of occupancy of Phase III of the project. The
City and EBI have discussed whether EBI would seek an amendment to its conditional use permit
to eliminate the condition to construct the Path. However, the City ultimately directed EBI to
construct the Path as required by the conditions of approval and pursuant to the design approved by
City Council in 2012. The City has also initiated a General Plan amendment to reinstate depiction

File No. A-16-002
Page No. 3

of the through-block connection and to amend the Pedestrian and Bicycle Plan to reinstate depiction
of the mid-block path.

Pursuant to the Government Code Section 65350 et seq. and Emeryville Municipal Code 9-
7.1201 et seq., the Planning Commission first considers the proposed General Plan and makes a
recommendation to the City Council. At its December 10, 2015, meeting, the Planning Commission
adopted a resolution recommending to the City Council amend the General Plan and Pedestrian and
Bicycle Plan to reinstate depiction of the pedestrian path connecting 45th Street and 47th Street
along the eastern property line of the EBI campus. The City Council will next consider whether to
adopt the proposed amendments to the General Plan and the Pedestrian and Bicycle Plan at an
upcoming meeting.

Mayor Martinez lives on 45th Street in a single family home, which she owns. The
boundary of her property to the boundary of the EBI Campus, as well as the proposed Path itself, is
approximately 472 feet. The Mayor's property is at the corner of Essex and 45th Streets just over a
block from the closest corner of the path.

The neighbors with properties abutting the Path to the east have commented during public
hearings that the path has the potential to create negative impacts on their properties related to
health and safety issues because they are directly adjacent to the proposed path. However, the
environmental review of EBI's conditional use permit indicates that the Path will have no
environmental impact. Furthermore, the purpose of the proposed path is to allow pedestrians to
continue to walk in the neighborhood by providing a mid-block crossing, instead of forcing
pedestrians to San Pablo Avenue to walk between 45th Street and 47th Street.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Different standards
apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material
depending on the nature of the interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are
defined in Section 87103.

Under Sections 82033 and 87103(b), an official has an interest in any real property owned
directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate family, if the
interest has a fair market value of $2,000 or more. For Mayor Martinez, the only interest identified
that may be implicated by the decisions in question is her real property interest in her residence.
Accordingly, we must determine whether the financial effect on her interest in her residence
resulting from the decisions is both foreseeable and material.

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably

File No. A-16-002
Page No. 4

foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest.
(Regulation 18702.) The materiality standards for any particular interest are provided in
Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5.

Based upon the facts provided, Mayor Martinez's real property interest is not explicitly
involved in decisions regarding the Path and a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect
can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. Moreover, any
reasonably foreseeable effect on a residential interest in real property is material whenever the
governmental decision affects real property located within 500 feet of the property line of the
official's property, unless the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that
there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the property. (Regulation
18702.2(a)(11).) Pursuant to Regulation 18702(b), the effect of a decision is not material if it is
"nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant."

As provided in the facts, the Path allows for a pedestrian route between 45th and 47th Streets
instead of forcing pedestrians to walk around EMI's campus to San Pablo Avenue. While neighbors
directly adjacent to the path have been vocal about the potential effects on abutting properties, it is
significant that Mayor Martinez's property is a little more than a block away from the closest corner
of the path and not adjacent to the path. Based upon the facts provided, it is rather unlikely that the
small improvement in the walkability of the Mayor's neighborhood will have more than nominal,
inconsequential, or insignificant effect on the residence. Accordingly, Mayor Martinez is not
prohibited from taking part in decisions regarding the City's General Plan or Pedestrian and Bicycle
Plan limited to the Path.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

sections.question:
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Mayor Martinez from taking part in 
decisions to amend the City's General Plan and Pedestrian and Bicycle plan to add a pedestrian path 
between 45th and 4t11 Streets that is within 500 feet of her residence?
sections.conclusion:
Mayor Martinez is not disqualified from decisions regarding the pedestrian path between 
45th and 4th Streets because it is unlikely that this small improvement in her neighborhood's 
walkability will have a measurable impact on her residence.

QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Mayor Martinez from taking part in 
decisions to amend the City's General Plan and Pedestrian and Bicycle plan to add a pedestrian path 
between 45th and 4t11 Streets that is within 500 feet of her residence?

CONCLUSION: Mayor Martinez is not disqualified from decisions regarding the pedestrian path between 
45th and 4th Streets because it is unlikely that this small improvement in her neighborhood's 
walkability will have a measurable impact on her residence.
